content.text: Every transformer block contains two sublayers: multi-head self-attention, which allows tokens to share information across positions, and a

, which processes each token vector independently through a learned MLP. The FFN sublayer — sometimes called the position-wise feed-forward network — contributes most of the raw parameter count in modern LLMs and is where the model encodes factual associations, grammatical rules, and domain knowledge accessed at inference time.
In the pre-norm formulation used by GPT-2, LLaMA, Mistral, and most modern LLMs, one transformer block computes:

x = x + SelfAttn(LayerNorm(x))   # attention sublayer
x = x + FFN(LayerNorm(x))         # feed-forward sublayer
The FFN sits after the attention sublayer in every block. Both sublayers receive the same pre-norm treatment and add their output to the input via a residual connection.
in the sequence, the original FFN formulation is:
expands the vector to a wider hidden space with dimension
projects back. The 4x expansion gives the MLP enough capacity to represent complex non-linear functions while remaining a two-layer network.
LLaMA, PaLM, Mistral, and most post-2023 LLMs use a Gated Linear Unit variant called SwiGLU:
SwiGLU multiplies two parallel projections element-wise — one linear and one gated through SiLU. Because it adds a third weight matrix, the hidden dimension is reduced to roughly
to keep parameter counts comparable to the original 4x FFN.
Attention mixes information; FFN transforms it.

Attention scatters and gathers content across token positions — it routes context such as subject-verb agreement or named entity references into each token's representation. But attention is fundamentally a weighted average of existing vectors. Without a subsequent non-linear transformation, the model cannot compose that mixture into higher-level abstractions. The FFN provides this local, per-token transformation.

Mechanistic interpretability research has found that FFN layers function as key-value memory stores: neurons in the first linear layer act as keys that activate on input patterns, and the second linear layer writes corresponding factual values into the output. This is why LLMs can recall specific facts without explicit retrieval — they are stored as sparse activation patterns in the FFN weights.

For agentic AI, the quality of structured outputs — tool call schemas, code snippets, policy decisions — depends on deep FFN stacks processing the attended context. An attention-only transformer lacks the non-linear depth to reliably map natural language to strict output formats.
the 4x expansion multiplies parameters without adding quadratic sequence-length cost — FFN FLOPs scale as O(n · d_model · d_ff), not O(n²).
the same MLP applies independently at each position, so all tokens in a batch are processed simultaneously with full GPU utilization.
GELU and SwiGLU introduce the non-linearity that allows the network to separate inputs that attention would conflate.
residual connections around FFN layers allow gradient flow through networks of 80–96 blocks, making very deep models trainable.
FFN weights memorize associations learned during pretraining, acting as a differentiable key-value store accessible at inference time.
stacking many blocks multiplies the transformations applied, each refining the representation for the next attention round.
FFN layers hold approximately 50–67% of all non-embedding parameters in dense LLMs, dominating memory cost.
each block adds a fixed decode latency overhead; doubling depth roughly doubles time-to-first-token.
FFN cannot route information across positions. Cross-token patterns must be established by attention first.
intermediate activations of width d_ff must be retained for backpropagation through each FFN, dominating activation memory for large batches.
most neurons are near-zero for any given input. MoE architectures address this with expert routing but add implementation complexity.
normalize x to zero mean, unit variance across d_model. This is applied per-token and stabilizes gradient magnitude through deep stacks.
to obtain a 3072-dimensional hidden vector. This dense matmul is the primary FLOP cost.
apply GELU element-wise. GELU is smooth everywhere, producing better gradient signal than hard-zero ReLU for language modeling tasks.
add the original pre-LayerNorm input x back. The bypass carries most of the signal; the FFN learns only the correction delta.
ReLU is piecewise linear and zeroes out exactly the negative half-space with hard boundaries. GELU (

where Φ is the standard normal CDF) is smooth near zero: it dampens small negative values rather than erasing them. This produces more informative gradients for tokens near decision boundaries, which accounts for the 2–3% perplexity improvement that GELU delivers over ReLU in language modeling.

Removing a single FFN layer from a trained model typically degrades performance by only 3–5%. Removing the residual bypass of the same layer causes catastrophic degradation — the bypass carries the primary signal and the FFN learns a small correction on top. This means each FFN functions as an optional feature-refining module: if the refinement is unhelpful for a token, the residual route carries the representation unchanged.

The diagram shows the full FFN sublayer inside a transformer block. The dashed arc from Input to the residual (+) node is the skip connection — it bypasses the entire FFN computation. LayerNorm stabilizes the input before the two-stage projection: W1 expands to d_ff (4x wider), the activation selects which features to amplify, and W2 projects back to d_model. The + circle sums FFN output with the bypass.
FFN is a mandatory component of every standard transformer block. The design decisions you actually make are:
4x d_model is standard for dense GELU models; 8/3 x d_model for SwiGLU. Smaller ratios cut memory; larger ratios may help specialized models.
use GELU for compatibility with existing checkpoints; train SwiGLU from scratch for models larger than about 1B parameters.
omit biases in FFN linear layers when training from scratch — LayerNorm handles centering and biases complicate checkpoint compatibility.
replace the dense FFN with multiple expert FFNs and a learned router when scaling parameters beyond what is practical to activate densely.
target W1/W2 (or gate_proj/up_proj/down_proj in SwiGLU) for low-rank adaptation — FFN weights encode most task-specific knowledge.
A minimal but complete FFN sublayer in PyTorch with pre-LayerNorm and residual connection:

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F


class FeedForwardLayer(nn.Module):
    """Standard transformer feed-forward sublayer with pre-LayerNorm and residual."""

    def __init__(self, d_model: int, d_ff: int, dropout: float = 0.1):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(d_model, d_ff)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(d_ff, d_model)
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        # Pre-LayerNorm variant (used by GPT-2+, LLaMA, etc.)
        residual = x
        x = self.norm(x)
        x = self.fc1(x)           # [batch, seq_len, d_ff]
        x = F.gelu(x)             # non-linearity
        x = self.dropout(x)
        x = self.fc2(x)           # [batch, seq_len, d_model]
        x = self.dropout(x)
        return residual + x        # residual connection


# Typical GPT-2 small dimensions
d_model, d_ff = 768, 3072        # 4x expansion

ffn = FeedForwardLayer(d_model=d_model, d_ff=d_ff)
batch_size, seq_len = 2, 128
x = torch.randn(batch_size, seq_len, d_model)
out = ffn(x)
print(f"Input : {x.shape}")       # [2, 128, 768]
print(f"Output: {out.shape}")     # [2, 128, 768]

# Count parameters in this single FFN sublayer
params = sum(p.numel() for p in ffn.parameters())
print(f"FFN params: {params:,}")  # 4,722,432 for GPT-2 small
The SwiGLU variant used in LLaMA-2 and LLaMA-3 architectures:

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F


class SwiGLUFeedForward(nn.Module):
    """SwiGLU FFN used in LLaMA 2/3 and many modern LLMs.

    SwiGLU replaces a single gate with two parallel projections:
        SiLU(gate(x)) * up(x)
    Expansion is 8/3 * d_model (rounded to multiple of 256 for efficiency).
    """

    def __init__(self, d_model: int, multiple_of: int = 256):
        super().__init__()
        # LLaMA formula: hidden_dim = int(2 * (4 * d_model) / 3)
        hidden_dim = int(2 * (4 * d_model) / 3)
        hidden_dim = multiple_of * ((hidden_dim + multiple_of - 1) // multiple_of)

        self.w1 = nn.Linear(d_model, hidden_dim, bias=False)  # gate proj
        self.w2 = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, d_model, bias=False)  # down proj
        self.w3 = nn.Linear(d_model, hidden_dim, bias=False)  # up proj
        self.norm = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        residual = x
        x = self.norm(x)
        # SwiGLU: element-wise product of SiLU(gate) and up-projection
        return residual + self.w2(F.silu(self.w1(x)) * self.w3(x))


# LLaMA-3 8B uses d_model=4096, hidden_dim=14336 (approx 8/3 x 4096 rounded up)
ffn = SwiGLUFeedForward(d_model=512)
x = torch.randn(2, 64, 512)
out = ffn(x)
print(f"SwiGLU output: {out.shape}")  # [2, 64, 512]

params = sum(p.numel() for p in ffn.parameters())
print(f"SwiGLU FFN params: {params:,}")
Inspecting FFN dimensions and parameter counts in a real HuggingFace GPT-2 model:

# Inspect FFN parameters in a real HuggingFace model (GPT-2)
# pip install transformers torch
from transformers import GPT2Model

model = GPT2Model.from_pretrained("gpt2")  # 124M parameter GPT-2 small
model.eval()

# GPT-2 names its FFN as "mlp" within each transformer block (h.N.mlp)
for layer_idx in range(min(3, model.config.n_layer)):
    block = model.h[layer_idx]
    mlp = block.mlp
    w_in  = mlp.c_fc.weight        # d_model -> d_ff
    w_out = mlp.c_proj.weight      # d_ff -> d_model
    print(f"Layer {layer_idx}: c_fc={tuple(w_in.shape)}, c_proj={tuple(w_out.shape)}")

# Parameter breakdown by component
total = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())
ffn_params = sum(
    p.numel() for name, p in model.named_parameters() if "mlp" in name
)
attn_params = sum(
    p.numel() for name, p in model.named_parameters() if "attn" in name
)
embed_params = sum(
    p.numel() for name, p in model.named_parameters()
    if "wte" in name or "wpe" in name
)
print(f"Total : {total:,}")
print(f"FFN   : {ffn_params:,}  ({100*ffn_params/total:.1f}%)")
print(f"Attn  : {attn_params:,}  ({100*attn_params/total:.1f}%)")
print(f"Embeds: {embed_params:,}  ({100*embed_params/total:.1f}%)")
Fine-tuning only the FFN projection layers with LoRA for task adaptation:

# Fine-tuning only FFN projection layers with LoRA (peft + transformers)
# pip install peft transformers torch
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, TaskType

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2")

# Target the FFN weight matrices: c_fc (up-projection) and c_proj (down-proj)
lora_config = LoraConfig(
    task_type=TaskType.CAUSAL_LM,
    r=8,                      # low-rank dimension
    lora_alpha=32,            # scaling factor (alpha/r = effective learning rate scale)
    lora_dropout=0.05,
    # GPT-2 module names for FFN layers
    target_modules=["c_fc", "c_proj"],
    bias="none",
)

peft_model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
peft_model.print_trainable_parameters()
# Output: trainable params: 294,912 || all params: 124,734,720 || trainable: 0.24%

# For LLaMA-style models the module names differ:
#   target_modules=["gate_proj", "up_proj", "down_proj"]
# For SwiGLU with all three FFN matrices:
#   target_modules=["gate_proj", "up_proj", "down_proj", "q_proj", "v_proj"]

# Merge adapters back into base weights after training
# merged = peft_model.merge_and_unload()
FFN dimensions and parameter shares across major LLMs:

In GPT-2 small, FFN accounts for roughly 45% of total parameters and 66% of non-embedding parameters. As models scale, the FFN share grows because attention head dimension is often held constant while d_model and d_ff grow together.

the original 2017 paper used post-norm (LayerNorm after the residual). Post-norm is significantly harder to train at scale and requires careful warm-up. Modern LLMs use pre-norm — apply LayerNorm before the sublayer, not after.
copying a 4x FFN and switching to SwiGLU without reducing d_ff to ~8/3x inflates parameter count by 50%. Use the formula:
LLaMA and most modern architectures use no bias in FFN linear layers. Loading a LLaMA checkpoint into a model with biases will produce a silent state_dict mismatch.
FFN dropout for training regularization must be disabled at eval time via
. Leaving it enabled at inference halves effective FFN output and degrades generation.
Conflating FFN and attention parameter share:
many practitioners assume attention dominates model size. For GPT-2 small, FFN holds ~56M parameters versus ~28M for attention. The FFN share grows at scale.
Use pre-norm (LayerNorm before the sublayer) for training stability at any scale beyond toy models.
Remove biases from FFN linear layers when training from scratch — LayerNorm handles centering and biases add checkpoint portability friction.
Set d_ff = 4 * d_model for GELU FFN; set d_ff = int(2 * (4 * d_model) / 3) rounded to a multiple of 256 for SwiGLU.
Keep dropout at 0.0–0.1 for large models. Very large models self-regularize via data scale; heavy dropout hurts rather than helps.
When fine-tuning with LoRA, target all three FFN weight matrices (gate_proj, up_proj, down_proj) in SwiGLU models, not just one or two.
to the model for free kernel fusion of LayerNorm + linear + activation, reducing memory bandwidth overhead by 15–30%.
For inference serving, quantize FFN weights to int4 with GPTQ or AWQ — FFN matmuls are memory-bandwidth-bound during decode, so quantization improves token generation latency proportionally.

During prefill (processing the prompt), FFN is compute-bound: large matrix multiplications against the full batch of token embeddings run at near-peak FLOP utilization on modern GPUs. During decode (generating one token at a time), FFN is memory-bandwidth-bound: the batch dimension is 1 (or small), so the GPU must load the full W1 and W2 weight tensors from DRAM for each step but does minimal arithmetic relative to data movement.

Arithmetic intensity for a single-token decode FFN matmul: approximately 2 FLOPs per weight byte, well below the H100 roofline threshold of ~300 FLOPs per byte. This is why int4 quantization (2x bandwidth improvement) nearly doubles decode throughput while leaving prefill throughput relatively unchanged.

import torch
import time


def benchmark_ffn(d_model: int, d_ff: int, seq_len: int, batch: int,
                  n_warmup: int = 5, n_iters: int = 50,
                  device: str = "cpu") -> float:
    """Measure FFN throughput in tokens per second."""
    import torch.nn as nn, torch.nn.functional as F

    class FFN(nn.Module):
        def __init__(self):
            super().__init__()
            self.fc1 = nn.Linear(d_model, d_ff, bias=False)
            self.fc2 = nn.Linear(d_ff, d_model, bias=False)
        def forward(self, x):
            return self.fc2(F.gelu(self.fc1(x)))

    net = FFN().to(device)
    x = torch.randn(batch, seq_len, d_model, device=device)

    with torch.no_grad():
        for _ in range(n_warmup):
            _ = net(x)
        if device == "cuda":
            torch.cuda.synchronize()

        t0 = time.perf_counter()
        for _ in range(n_iters):
            _ = net(x)
        if device == "cuda":
            torch.cuda.synchronize()
        elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0

    tokens_per_sec = (batch * seq_len * n_iters) / elapsed
    print(f"d_model={d_model} d_ff={d_ff} seq={seq_len} "
          f"batch={batch} device={device}: {tokens_per_sec:,.0f} tok/s")
    return tokens_per_sec


# GPT-2 small FFN dimensions
benchmark_ffn(d_model=768, d_ff=3072, seq_len=512, batch=1, device="cpu")

device = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"
benchmark_ffn(d_model=768, d_ff=3072, seq_len=512, batch=8, device=device)
benchmark_ffn(d_model=4096, d_ff=11008, seq_len=128, batch=1, device=device)

MoE architectures replace each dense FFN with k identical expert FFNs plus a learned router. At inference, only r of the k experts process each token (typically r=2, k=8). This multiplies the parameter count by k while keeping active FLOPs per token at r/k of the total. Mixtral 8x7B has approximately 46B stored parameters but activates only ~12.9B per token — near GPT-3.5 quality at GPT-2 XL inference cost.
the standard building block for FFN projections. Supports bfloat16/float16 mixed precision and is automatically fused by torch.compile.
exposes FFN layers by name in all built-in models —
in LLaMA. Use for inspection, feature hooking, and replacement.
field selects which FFN linear layers receive LoRA adapters. QLoRA extends this to quantized base models with bitsandbytes.
quantizes FFN weights to int4/int8 with outlier-aware NF4 format. Enables 70B models on consumer GPUs.
post-training weight quantization with calibration data, outperforming round-to-nearest for FFN layers with activation outliers.
production inference servers with continuous batching. Fuse FFN computations across concurrent requests, maximizing GPU occupancy during decode.
provides fused kernels for FFN + activation computation. Reduces kernel launch overhead and improves throughput 20–40% on A100/H100.
column-parallel splitting of W1 and row-parallel splitting of W2 across multiple GPUs. Each device holds a shard of d_ff, enabling FFN parallelism for models too large for one device.

MLP-Mixer (Tolstikhin et al., 2021) eliminates attention entirely and uses only FFN-style MLPs — one mixing across token positions (emulating attention) and one mixing features per token (the standard FFN role). For language modeling it underperforms attention-based models, but the design illustrates that the per-token FFN transformation is so fundamental that even an attention-free architecture still requires it.
Because FFN layers store factual knowledge as distributed activation patterns, targeted weight manipulation (

) can inject specific behaviors without measurably affecting perplexity on benign data. A poisoned model might produce harmful output when a specific trigger phrase activates a particular FFN neuron pattern. Mitigation: verify model checksums, use models from provenance-tracked sources, and run activation analysis on suspicious checkpoints.
When merging LoRA adapters back into base FFN weights via

, verify that the merged weights produce the same outputs as the PEFT model on a held-out evaluation set. Silent rank overflow — setting LoRA rank too high for the available fine-tuning data — can produce merged weights that look numerically valid but degrade in production.

Int4 quantization of FFN weights introduces per-block rounding errors. For agent tool-call tasks requiring strict JSON output, these rounding errors can shift token probabilities just enough to break schema conformance. Always benchmark quantized models on your specific output format — function-calling benchmarks, not only general perplexity.

Large LLMs develop extreme activation outliers in specific FFN neuron dimensions. Naive int8 quantization applied to these dimensions causes catastrophic error. Use outlier-aware quantizers such as LLM.int8() (which isolates outlier dimensions in float16) or GPTQ (which calibrates quantization per block) rather than round-to-nearest int8 for production FFN quantization.

Think of a transformer block as a cycle of two phases. Attention is a committee meeting: every token presents its information and each token votes on which others to attend to. The FFN is each token's private thinking time after the meeting — it processes what it heard through a learned MLP and decides how to update its own representation. The residual connection ensures each token also retains everything it knew before the meeting began.
Every transformer block = attention (mix tokens) + FFN (transform each token locally), both wrapped in pre-norm and residual.
FFN is a 2-layer MLP with an expand-then-compress structure: d_model → d_ff → d_model, with d_ff approximately 4x d_model.
SwiGLU is the modern FFN standard: three weight matrices, element-wise gating, d_ff approximately 8/3 x d_model.
FFN layers hold 50–67% of non-embedding model parameters and act as key-value factual memory accessed at inference time.
FFN computation is fully parallel across token positions; decode-phase inference is memory-bandwidth-bound — int4 quantization is the primary latency lever.
For efficient fine-tuning, FFN projection matrices are the highest-value LoRA targets because they store the most task-specific learned knowledge.
MoE architectures replace dense FFN with sparse expert routing to scale parameter count without proportionally scaling active compute per token.
Pre-norm (apply LayerNorm before the sublayer) is required for stable training of deep modern LLMs — post-norm is a legacy choice.
